mappings.rationale: Covers prehistoric cultures and their material production, directly relevant to arts of the Stone Age peoples (A).
Covers rock art, Lascaux, and archaeological methods essential for studying Stone Age arts and ancient civilizations (A).
Directly covers arts of ancient Egypt, explicitly named in the outline of Western arts in antiquity (B.1).
Provides cultural context for ancient Egyptian arts including museums, religion, and writing systems (B.1).
Egyptian hieroglyphic writing is a distinctive art form of ancient Egypt, covering script and visual expression (B.1).
Directly covers the ancient Middle East civilizations whose arts are explicitly listed in the outline (B.1).
Assyrian palace reliefs and art constitute a major tradition within the ancient Middle East arts (B.1).
Babylonian arts and writing are part of the ancient Middle East artistic tradition in the outline (B.1).
Section B.1 explicitly covers ancient Greek and Hellenistic arts and ancient Roman arts; Classics provides the foundational cultural context for understanding these artistic traditions.
Covers Greek and Latin literature -- epic, lyric, satire, drama -- as key art forms of classical antiquity (B.1).
Greek and Roman mythological subjects were central to ancient visual and literary arts (B.1).
Provides the cultural context for ancient Greek arts explicitly named in the outline (B.1).
Covers the Hellenistic period whose arts are explicitly named in the outline of Western antiquity (B.1).
Covers Roman civilization whose arts are explicitly listed under Western arts of antiquity (B.1).
Covers the transition from Roman to Early Christian arts, both explicitly named in the outline (B.1).
Directly covers Early Christian art and medieval Christian artistic traditions in the Western canon (B.1, B.2).
Byzantine art bridges Roman/Early Christian arts and medieval Western arts, with its own distinctive artistic tradition (B.1, B.2).
Directly relevant to the outline's coverage of arts of the Middle Ages in the Western tradition (B.2).
Covers medieval literary arts including poetry and courtly love literature in the Western tradition (B.2).
Directly relevant to the outline's coverage of Renaissance arts in Europe (B.3).
Specifically covers Renaissance visual arts including painting, sculpture, and architecture in Europe (B.3).
Covers baroque and Renaissance music as Western art forms from the Renaissance period onward (B.3).
Covers Romantic-era arts across literature, music, and painting in Europe, part of the Renaissance-to-present trajectory (B.3).
Covers modern visual arts in Europe, part of the outline's scope from Renaissance to present (B.3).
Covers the most recent global art developments, completing the timeline from Renaissance to present (B.3).
Covers major 20th-century European art movements in the Western tradition (B.3).
Covers European avant-garde art movements from the 19th-20th centuries (B.3).
Covers the Italian Futurist movement in art and literature, part of 20th-century European arts (B.3).
Covers modern architectural arts in Europe and America from the Renaissance to present (B.3).
Covers British architectural arts as part of the European Western tradition (B.3).
Covers a major American arts movement blending African and American traditions (B.3).
Covers the development of American arts and print culture, relevant to Western arts in America (B.3).
Directly covers a major art form of China, explicitly listed under East Asian arts (C.1).
Covers Japanese literary arts and aesthetics, directly relevant to East Asian arts -- Japan (C.1).
Confucian aesthetics and literary traditions profoundly shaped arts of China, Japan, and Korea (C.1).
Korea is explicitly listed alongside China and Japan in the outline of East Asian arts (C.1).
China is explicitly named in the outline of East Asian arts (C.1), and modern Chinese cultural developments shaped contemporary Chinese arts.
Japan is explicitly named in the outline of East Asian arts (C.1), and modern Japanese culture shaped contemporary Japanese artistic expression.
Covers samurai culture and its distinctive artistic traditions in Japan (C.1).
Covers Mongol civilization and its arts, relevant to Central Asian arts and nomadic peoples (C.2).
Covers artistic and cultural exchange across Central, East, and South Asia along major trade routes (C.1, C.2, C.3).
Tibetan Buddhist art is central to the arts of Tibet and Himalayan countries under Central Asian arts (C.2).
Hindu traditions are deeply intertwined with the visual, performing, and literary arts of India and South Asia (C.3).
Covers a major modern art form of India, relevant to the arts of South Asian peoples (C.3).
India is explicitly named in the outline of South Asian arts (C.3), and modern India's cultural movements shaped contemporary Indian arts.
Pakistan is explicitly listed in the outline of South Asian arts (C.3: India, Sri Lanka, Kashmir, Pakistan, Bangladesh).
Indian philosophical and aesthetic traditions shaped the arts of South Asia including India and Sri Lanka (C.3).
Directly relevant to the outline's coverage of arts of Southeast Asian peoples including Burma, Cambodia, and Indonesia (C.4).
Iranian art is a major tradition within the arts of the Middle East and Islamic peoples explicitly covered in section D, including Persian miniatures, architecture, and calligraphy.
Islamic artistic traditions -- calligraphy, architecture, geometric design -- are central to arts of the Islamic peoples (D).
Section D explicitly covers arts of the Middle East and of the Islamic peoples, and Islamic civilization's history shaped artistic traditions across the Muslim world.
Section D explicitly covers arts of the Islamic peoples, and Islamic cultural traditions span the Middle East, North Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia covered in sections C-D.
Relevant to the outline's explicit coverage of arts of the Jewish peoples (D.1).
Section D.1 explicitly covers arts of the Jewish peoples, and Jewish historical experience shaped artistic and literary traditions across diaspora communities.
Directly covers literature as an art form of the Jewish peoples, explicitly listed in the outline (D.1).
Covers African art and cultural history across Sudanic, Central, East, Southern, and West African regions listed in the outline (E).
African religious rituals, cosmology, and sacred arts are deeply connected to artistic traditions of African peoples (E).
Covers Australian Aboriginal culture and land rights, relevant to arts of the Australian Aboriginal peoples (F.4).
Directly relevant to the outline's coverage of arts of Eskimo and North American Indian peoples (G.1).
Covers literature as an art form of North American Indian peoples (G.1).
Covers Aztec civilization including sacrificial art, codices, and monumental architecture -- arts of Meso-American peoples (G.2).
Covers Mayan artistic expression and city-states, directly relevant to arts of Meso-American peoples (G.2).
Covers baroque literary arts blending indigenous and colonial traditions in Meso-America and South America (G.2, G.3).
Covers modern literary arts of Latin America, relevant to the continuation of Meso-American and South American artistic traditions (G.2, G.3).
Covers folk musical traditions across cultures, relevant to the cross-cultural survey of arts in particular societies (A through G).
Covers musical traditions across global cultures, relevant to the section's survey of arts in diverse societies (A through G).
Provides methodology for studying music across the diverse cultures covered throughout this section (A through G).
Provides the overarching framework for understanding art across different cultures and historical periods throughout the section.
Covers French cinema as a major European art form, relevant to Western arts from the Renaissance to present (B.3).
Traces the bohemian movement and its influence on artistic counter-cultures from Romanticism to the Beats, relevant to B.2 (arts of the Western tradition) and modern artistic subcultures.
